title: SAS Unveils Quantum Lab on Viya Platform, Combining Emulated Workloads with a "Physics-First" Auditing Approach - Quantum Computing Report
gist: SAS launched Quantum Lab inside its Viya platform, a classical-first emulation environment that auto-tunes quantum algorithms on classical CAS workers to claim 100x speedup and 99% cost savings before touching real hardware, arguing (per its survey of 500+ execs) that use-case uncertainty, not cost, is now the top quantum adoption barrier.

insights.claim: SAS's 2026 survey of over 500 technology executives found that uncertainty about practical, real-world business use cases has surpassed capital expense as the primary quantum adoption barrier.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: If the bottleneck has moved from cost to proven use cases, value accrues to whoever can demonstrate ROI use cases, not just cheaper hardware access.
insights.quote: uncertainty around practical, real-world business use cases has surpassed capital expense as the primary adoption barrier in the post-classical space
insights.locator: The software rollout coincides with the publication

insights.claim: In 2025 the top enterprise bottlenecks were raw implementation costs (38%) and a basic lack of comprehension (35%), but by 2026 leaders understand quantum AI and are instead holding back out of fear investments won't yield measurable utility.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: The year-over-year shift from ignorance to ROI-skepticism signals a maturing but still pre-revenue market; adoption catalysts will be demonstrated payback, not education.
insights.locator: A Structural Shift in Enterprise Adoption Barriers

insights.claim: Quantum Lab is a software abstraction layer, not a quantum machine, using SAS Viya's Cloud Analytic Services (CAS) workers to emulate quantum environments classically and parallelize parameter tuning.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Positioning as classical emulation reframes SAS as complementary to, not competing with, quantum hardware vendors, expanding its addressable market without owning a QPU.
insights.quote: The strategic architecture of Quantum Lab is intentionally designed as a software abstraction layer rather than a standalone quantum machine.
insights.locator: The strategic architecture of Quantum Lab

insights.claim: Because refining quantum algorithms can rack up hundreds of thousands of dollars in real hardware runtime fees, emulating auto-tuning classically lets firms test thousands of permutations for the cost of a standard software license.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: The core commercial pitch: replace metered QPU spend with a fixed software license, a compelling cost story while hardware runtime remains expensive.
insights.quote: a process that can rack up hundreds of thousands of dollars in real-world hardware runtime fees
insights.locator: Because refining quantum algorithms requires iterating

insights.claim: Internal benchmarks show the emulation loop delivers a 100-fold development speedup and 99% cost reduction prior to actual quantum processor deployment.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Eye-catching but self-reported internal figures; the 100x/99% claims are marketing benchmarks to validate independently before underwriting them.
insights.quote: this emulation loop delivers a 100-fold development speedup and a 99% cost reduction prior to actual quantum processor deployment

insights.claim: Quantum Lab, announced at SAS Innovate in Dallas, is slated for general commercial availability in Q4 2026 and abstracts hardware constraints like qubit connectivity so non-physicists can compile workflows, with a virtual quantum AI tutor that generates code and resolves syntax errors.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A Q4 2026 GA date and non-physicist accessibility target the broad enterprise developer base, the addressable-market expansion move for SAS.

insights.claim: SAS pre-audits all corporate problem submissions classically before recommending quantum; in one case an insurance group's optimization challenge was solved classically in under two minutes just by reformulating the dataset, avoiding quantum hardware entirely.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A vendor telling customers most problems don't need quantum is a credibility play that also gates real quantum spend to genuinely hard combinatorial cases.
insights.quote: an optimization challenge submitted by an insurance group was successfully resolved classically in under two minutes by simply reformulating the underlying dataset
insights.locator: Crucially, Wisotsky emphasizes a strict, non-dogmatic auditing approach

insights.claim: Valid quantum use cases are highly combinatorial ones like financial fraud pattern recognition, portfolio rebalancing, and logistics routing, where teams must shift mindset from classical data science to physics.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Names the specific verticals (finance, logistics) where quantum ROI is most plausible, a targeting guide for where near-term demand will concentrate.
insights.locator: For valid, highly combinatorial use cases

insights.claim: Whereas classical data science flags highly correlated variables as problematic collinearity, quantum systems actively harness these interdependencies via state entanglement to navigate solution spaces classical systems cannot efficiently parse.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A crisp mental model for where quantum advantage lives: the very variable interdependencies classical methods discard become quantum's exploitable structure.
insights.quote: quantum systems actively harness these deep interdependencies via state entanglement to navigate expansive solution spaces that classical systems cannot efficiently parse
insights.locator: While traditional data science flags highly correlated variables
